title: Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil

seo.title: Rosemary Focaccia Genovese Recipe | Authentic Italian Bread
seo.metaDescription: Bake authentic Rosemary Focaccia Genovese with this easy recipe. Enjoy the classic Italian bread, fragrant with rosemary and rich olive oil.
longTailKeywords: how to make Rosemary Focaccia Genovese
Rosemary Focaccia Genovese step by step
best Rosemary Focaccia Genovese at home
lowCompetitionKeywords: Genovese focaccia with rosemary and olive oil
h2Headings: Pro Tips for the Best Rosemary Focaccia
seo.imageAlt: Freshly baked Rosemary Focaccia Genovese with olive oil and rosemary sprigs on a cutting board.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy the irresistible aroma and taste of homemade Rosemary Focaccia Genovese, a classic Italian bread perfect for any meal.

description: Experience the taste of Liguria with this authentic Focaccia Genovese, a beloved Italian flatbread known for its irresistible chewiness and golden crust. Infused with fragrant rosemary and generous drizzles of extra virgin olive oil, it's a simple yet profoundly satisfying treat perfect for any occasion. This recipe brings the essence of a traditional Italian bakery right into your kitchen.

ingredients.notes: plus extra for brushing and drizzling

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a large bowl, combine the warm water and sugar. Sprinkle the active dry yeast over the top and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy. This indicates the yeast is active.

instructions.title: Mix the Dough
instructions.text: Add the bread flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Stir with a wooden spoon or a stand mixer with a dough hook until a shaggy dough forms. Drizzle in 2 tablespoons (30ml) of the extra virgin olive oil and mix until incorporated.

instructions.title: Knead and First Proof
instructions.text: Transfer the dough to a lightly floured surface and knead for about 8-10 minutes until it becomes smooth and elastic. Alternatively, use a stand mixer for 5-7 minutes. Form the dough into a ball, lightly grease the bowl with olive oil, place the dough back in, turn to coat, cover with plastic wrap, and let rise in a warm place for 1 to 1.5 hours, or until doubled in size.

instructions.title: Prepare Pan and Shape Dough
instructions.text: Generously grease a 9x13 inch (23x33 cm) baking pan with 2 tablespoons (30ml) of extra virgin olive oil, ensuring the sides are also coated. Gently transfer the risen dough to the prepared pan. With oiled hands, gently stretch the dough to fill the pan, being careful not to deflate it completely.

instructions.title: Second Proof and Dimpling
instructions.text: Cover the pan loosely with plastic wrap or a clean kitchen towel and let it rise again in a warm place for 30-45 minutes, or until noticeably puffy. Preheat your oven to 425F (220C). Once risen, remove the cover and use your oiled fingertips to gently create deep dimples all over the surface of the focaccia.

instructions.title: Top and Bake
instructions.text: Drizzle the remaining extra virgin olive oil generously over the dimpled focaccia. Sprinkle with the chopped fresh rosemary and flaky sea salt. Bake for 25-35 minutes, or until golden brown on top and cooked through. The internal temperature should be around 200-210F (93-99C).

instructions.title: Cool and Serve
instructions.text: Once baked, remove the focaccia from the oven. If desired, drizzle with a little more fresh olive oil for extra flavor and shine. Let it cool in the pan for 5-10 minutes before transferring to a wire rack to cool completely. Slice and serve warm or at room temperature.

tips: For an extra authentic touch, use a good quality Italian extra virgin olive oil, especially for the final drizzle. It makes a significant difference in flavor.
Do not rush the proofing process. Allowing the dough to rise fully in both stages is crucial for a light, airy, and chewy focaccia.
Focaccia is best enjoyed fresh on the day it's baked. If you have leftovers, store them at room temperature in an airtight container for up to 2 days, or freeze for longer storage.
article:
The aroma of freshly baked bread, infused with fragrant rosemary and rich olive oil, is one of life's simple pleasures. When that bread is a light, airy, and impossibly delicious Focaccia Genovese, the experience is elevated to an art form. This iconic Italian flatbread, originating from Genoa in the Liguria region, is a testament to the beauty of simple, high-quality ingredients. Unlike its thicker, breadier cousins, true Focaccia Genovese is characterized by its thin, crisp crust, an incredibly soft and open crumb, and a generous slick of golden olive oil that imparts both flavor and an irresistible texture. It's a culinary journey to the sun-drenched coast of Italy, right from your own kitchen. Today, we're diving deep into crafting the perfect Rosemary Focaccia Genovese, a staple of Italian baking that will undoubtedly become a favorite in your home.

## What is Focaccia Genovese?
Focaccia Genovese, often referred to simply as "fugassa" in the local dialect, is more than just bread; it's a cultural icon from Genoa. Its distinguishing features set it apart from other focaccia varieties you might encounter. While many focaccias are thick and spongy, the Genovese version is traditionally thinner, aiming for a delicate balance between a slightly crisp exterior and a supremely tender, almost cloud-like interior. The magic happens through high hydration, a long fermentation process, and the liberal application of good quality extra virgin olive oil – not just in the dough, but generously drizzled over the top before baking. The signature dimples, created by pressing fingertips into the risen dough, serve not only as a beautiful pattern but also as little pools to hold the precious olive oil and flaky sea salt. This creates pockets of intense flavor and a delightful textural contrast. Our focus today is on the classic Rosemary Focaccia Genovese, where the aromatic herb is lovingly pressed into those dimples, creating an unparalleled sensory experience.

## Ingredients for Rosemary Focaccia Genovese
Crafting an authentic Rosemary Focaccia Genovese requires a few key ingredients, each playing a vital role in achieving that perfect texture and flavor. Quality matters here, especially for the olive oil and rosemary.

*   **Strong Bread Flour (Type "00" or similar high-protein flour):** 500g. The high protein content develops strong gluten, essential for the focaccia’s chewy texture and open crumb structure.
*   **Lukewarm Water:** 400ml. The high hydration is crucial for a tender, airy focaccia.
*   **Active Dry Yeast:** 7g (1 packet). The leavening agent that gives the bread its rise.
*   **Fine Sea Salt:** 10g (for the dough). Balances the flavors and controls yeast activity.
*   **Extra Virgin Olive Oil:** 100ml (plus extra for oiling the pan and generous drizzling). Use a good quality, fruity olive oil; it's a star ingredient.
*   **Fresh Rosemary Sprigs:** 2-3 sprigs. For infusing that unmistakable aromatic flavor.
*   **Flaky Sea Salt:** For sprinkling on top. Adds a delightful crunch and burst of salinity.

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Focaccia
Making Rosemary Focaccia Genovese is a rewarding process that emphasizes patience and gentle handling.

1.  **Activate the Yeast:** In a large bowl, combine the lukewarm water and yeast. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy, indicating the yeast is active.
2.  **Mix the Dough:** Add the flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Stir with a wooden spoon or your hands until a shaggy, sticky dough forms. Add 2 tablespoons of the extra virgin olive oil and mix until just incorporated. The dough will be very wet – this is normal and desired for a high-hydration focaccia.
3.  **First Rise (Bulk Fermentation):** Cover the bowl tightly with plastic wrap or a damp kitchen towel. Let the dough rise at room temperature for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until it has at least doubled in size and is visibly bubbly. For a deeper flavor, you can also refrigerate it overnight (8-12 hours) after this initial mixing, then bring it back to room temperature before proceeding.
4.  **Prepare the Pan:** Generously grease a 9x13 inch (23x33 cm) baking pan with at least 3-4 tablespoons of extra virgin olive oil, ensuring the sides are well coated.
5.  **Transfer and Stretch:** Gently pour the risen dough into the prepared pan. It will be very soft and elastic. Using oiled hands, gently dimple and stretch the dough to fill the pan, being careful not to degas it too much. If the dough resists stretching, let it rest for 10-15 minutes, then continue. This gentle stretching helps maintain the airy structure.
6.  **Second Rise (Proofing):** Once the dough roughly fills the pan, cover it loosely with plastic wrap or a damp towel. Let it rise again in a warm place for another 45-60 minutes, or until it looks puffy and has nearly doubled in size again. Preheat your oven to 425°F (220°C) during the last 20 minutes of this rise.
7.  **Dimple and Top:** Uncover the dough. Generously drizzle the remaining extra virgin olive oil (at least 4-5 tablespoons) over the surface. Using your fingertips, gently but firmly press deep dimples all over the dough, ensuring some olive oil pools in these indentations. Strip the fresh rosemary leaves from their stems and scatter them over the focaccia, pressing some into the dimples. Sprinkle generously with flaky sea salt. This is the moment your Rosemary Focaccia Genovese truly comes to life.
8.  **Bake:** Place the focaccia in the preheated oven. Bake for 20-25 minutes, or until the top is golden brown and the bottom is crisp. You can carefully lift an edge to check the bottom.
9.  **Cool:** Once baked, remove the focaccia from the oven. If desired, you can drizzle a little more olive oil over the hot focaccia for extra richness. Carefully transfer it to a wire rack to cool slightly before slicing and serving.

## Pro Tips for the Best Rosemary Focaccia
Achieving the perfect Rosemary Focaccia Genovese is a delightful journey, and a few expert tips can elevate your results from good to truly exceptional.

*   **Embrace High Hydration:** The high water content in the dough is crucial for that characteristic airy, open crumb. Don't be afraid of a sticky dough; it's a sign you're on the right track. Oiling your hands helps immensely when handling it.
*   **Quality Olive Oil is Non-Negotiable:** Since olive oil is such a prominent flavor, invest in a good quality extra virgin olive oil. It makes a significant difference in the taste and texture of your finished focaccia, contributing to both a crisp crust and a rich, moist interior.
*   **Patience with Proofing:** Don't rush the rising times. The longer the dough proofs (within reason), the more flavor it develops, and the more airy the final product will be. A slow, cold fermentation (overnight in the fridge for the first rise) can yield even more complex flavors.
*   **Gentle Handling:** When transferring and stretching the dough, be gentle. You want to preserve as much of the air bubbles created during fermentation as possible. Use your fingertips to stretch and dimple, rather than punching it down.
*   **Don't Skimp on the Olive Oil and Salt:** The generous amount of olive oil on top, pooling in the dimples, is a hallmark of Focaccia Genovese. It fries the surface, creating an incredibly crisp and flavorful crust. The flaky sea salt adds a necessary textural contrast and burst of salinity.
*   **Preheat Your Pan (Optional but Recommended):** For an extra crispy bottom, you can preheat your empty baking pan in the oven for 10-15 minutes before carefully pouring in the dough and topping it. This creates an immediate sizzle effect.

## Serving Suggestions
The beauty of Rosemary Focaccia Genovese lies in its versatility. It's delicious on its own, but also acts as a fantastic canvas for various culinary pairings.

*   **As is:** Enjoy a warm slice plain, allowing the pure flavors of olive oil, rosemary, and salt to shine. It's perfect with a cup of coffee for breakfast or as a simple snack.
*   **Dipping:** Serve with a small bowl of high-quality extra virgin olive oil, perhaps with a splash of balsamic vinegar, for dipping.
*   **Charcuterie and Cheese Board:** Its mild flavor and delightful texture make it an excellent accompaniment to cured meats, artisanal cheeses, and olives.
*   **Soup or Salad Companion:** It’s wonderful for sopping up broths from hearty soups or as a side to a fresh green salad.
*   **Sandwiches:** Slice it horizontally and fill it with your favorite sandwich ingredients – prosciutto, mozzarella, roasted vegetables, or a simple tomato and basil combination.
*   **Bruschetta Base:** Toast slightly and top with fresh tomatoes, garlic, and basil for a twist on classic bruschetta.

## Variations and Substitutions
While the classic Rosemary Focaccia Genovese is perfect, there's always room for creative variations to suit your taste.

*   **Other Herbs:** While rosemary is traditional, feel free to experiment with other fresh herbs like thyme, oregano, or a mix of Italian herbs.
*   **Add-ins:** Before baking, you can press various toppings into the dimples along with the rosemary. Think cherry tomatoes (halved), thinly sliced red onion, black olives, or even thinly sliced potatoes.
*   **Garlic Infusion:** Mince a few cloves of garlic and mix them into the olive oil you drizzle over the focaccia for an extra aromatic kick.
*   **Cheese:** A sprinkle of grated Parmesan cheese or crumbled Gorgonzola can be added during the last 5-10 minutes of baking, or after it comes out of the oven.
*   **Sweet Focaccia:** While less common for the Genovese style, focaccia can be made sweet with sugar, fruit, or even chocolate chips.
*   **Flour Substitutions:** While strong bread flour is ideal, you can use all-purpose flour, though the texture might be slightly less chewy. Whole wheat flour can be used for a portion of the white flour, but it will result in a denser loaf.
*   **Gluten-Free:** For those with gluten sensitivities, a gluten-free version of focaccia is possible using specialized gluten-free flour blends, though the texture will naturally differ from traditional wheat-based Rosemary Focaccia Genovese.

The journey of baking Rosemary Focaccia Genovese is more than just following a recipe; it's an experience of connecting with a rich culinary tradition. The simple act of combining flour, water, yeast, olive oil, and fresh rosemary transforms into something truly magical. The aroma filling your kitchen, the golden crust, the tender interior – it's a sensory delight that speaks of comfort, warmth, and the timeless appeal of Italian baking. Whether enjoyed on its own or as part of a larger meal, this focaccia is sure to bring a touch of Ligurian sunshine to your table. So, roll up your sleeves, embrace the stickiness, and get ready to bake a masterpiece!
